nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Intro to Ecological Economics
nodes.description: Introduction to the study of economics as dependent on social and environmental systems and to transdisciplinary problem-solving using ecological economics. Prerequisite: Minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Environmental Law
nodes.description: An introduction to the dynamic and interdisciplinary field of environmental law in the United States. Examines the history of federal and state involvement in environmental decision-making and the most critical environmental problems we face today, including issues related to air and water pollution, biodiversity protection, and climate change. Prerequisites: NR 2040, POLS 1013, or POLS 1300; minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Junior standing

nodes.title: Solutions in Enviro Studies
nodes.description: Analysis and critique of grand challenges in environmental studies with an emphasis on understanding and solving pervasive global and local environmental problems such as global climate change.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for an ENVS 1510 A01 - A03 discussion section; Fee: $15.00; Open to Degree and PACE students

nodes.title: General Chemistry 2
nodes.description: Second semester of a two-semester sequence. Topics include solutions, kinetics, equilibrium, acid-base chemistry, aqueous ionic equilibria, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, and nuclear chemistry. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1450 and CHEM 1455. Prerequisite: CHEM 1400 or CHEM 1405.
nodes.other_requirements: $60 fee for lab supplies; Degree students only;

nodes.title: Human Health & the Environment
nodes.description: Offers an introduction to environmental health. Topics include: methods (toxicology, epidemiology) environmental health hazards (physical, biological, chemical) and supports (nature contact), risk analysis, communication and management, health and climate change, food production and access, energy production, and water. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing. Cross-listed with: HLTH 2070.
nodes.other_requirements: Sophomore standing.

nodes.title: INR Planning
nodes.description: Students design a program of study for the Integrated Natural Resources (INR) concentration in the Sustainability, Ecology & Policy major. Upon successful completion, students will have a program of study for their INR concentration.

nodes.title: Environmental Justice
nodes.description: Examines the historical trajectory of environmental justice; key lessons from EJ movements; the links between environmental justice, sustainability, decolonial movements, and just transitions; as well as how racism, classism, prejudice, and power are intimately intertwined with epistemic practices in science, technology, and environmental governance. Prerequisite: ENVS 1500, ENVS 1510, NR 1020, NR 1090, or ENSC 1010.

nodes.title: Sustainability Science
nodes.description: The study of sustainability integrating natural and social science perspectives. Topics include theories of ecological adaptation and resilience, sustainability assessment methods, life cycle analysis, relational values, community science, emerging technologies and their applications to achieving a sustainability transformation. Prerequisites: ENVS 1500, ENVS 1510, NR 1010, NR 1020, NR 1090, or Instructor permission; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission; minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required and completion of an Experiential Learning plan required

nodes.title: Energy and Climate Law
nodes.description: Focus at the intersection of energy law, environmental law, and climate law, including the regulations that empower government agencies to enforce the laws, and the policies that implement this enforcement. Partnership with Vermont Law School's Institute for Energy and the Environment provides an experiential learning opportunity for students. Prerequisite: Minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Junior standing. Course Fee: $24.00

nodes.title: Water as a Natural Resource
nodes.description: Uses of water resources and impacts on aquatic systems and human society. Prerequisites: Minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Social Proc & the Environment
nodes.description: Social science theories and their application to environmental issues. Analysis of issues using theories of government, economics, and social movements. Emphasis on integrating frameworks to analyze environmental issues. Prerequisite: NR 1020 or NR 1090.
nodes.other_requirements: RSENR students only; Minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Accelerated Biology
nodes.description: Selected topics from the full year of introductory biology, compressed into one semester. For students with demonstrated mastery of basic biology (e.g., AP credit). Permission required. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1425 and BCOR 1400 or BIOL 1400. Pre/co-requisite: CHEM 1400, CHEM 1405, or CHEM 1410.
nodes.other_requirements: For students with demonstrated mastery of basic biology (e.g., AP credit). Permission required. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1425 and BCOR 1400 or BIOL 1400. By invitation only; for FTFY students with AP score of 4. Instructor permission required. Must register for a BCOR 1425 lab. Must be enrolled in CHEM 1400 if have no CHEM credit.

nodes.title: Biodiversity Law
nodes.description: Covers laws and regulations that have been designed specifically to maintain and promote biodiversity, and those that, although not intended to, effectively contribute to biodiversity protection. Biodiversity issues can be both local and international at the same time, such as animal species that migrate between winter and summer feeding and breeding grounds. This means that local ordinances, state laws, national laws, and international compacts all can impact a single species.

nodes.title: Outline: General Chem w/lab
nodes.description: One-semester survey of principles and concepts of general chemistry, topics covered include bonding, mole ratios, equilibrium, and nuclear chemistry. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1102, CHEM 1400, or CHEM 1405.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1102, CHEM 1400, or CHEM 1405. $60 fee for lab supplies; Colocated with CHEM 1102A; Must register for CHEM 1100 lab L01-L07; Non-CAS, non - BSAD students only; Open to degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Ecology, Ecosystems & Environ
nodes.description: Major ecological concepts and their application. Analysis of form, structure, and function of organisms, populations, communities, ecosystems, and landscapes. Prerequisites: RSENR students or Agroecology or Landscape Design or Environmental Studies majors; minimum Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Landscape Ecology
nodes.description: Examines the critical role of landscape pattern in determining ecological process and dynamics, as well as human-ecological interactions. Includes field labs. Prerequisites: NR 2030 or BCOR 2100; Senior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Senior standing.

nodes.title: Undergraduate Research
nodes.description: Undergraduate student work on individual or small team research projects under the supervision of a faculty member for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Application and Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Women's Health and Environment
nodes.description: Uses interdisciplinary approaches to analyze specific connections between human-environment interactions from the gender perspective, especially women's and children's health. Examines the tensions between science, politics, gender and nature. Explores historical and contemporary understandings of gender in science and society at large. Prerequisite: Minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Junior standing

nodes.title: CL:FoundationsinPlace-BasedEdu
nodes.description: Introduces the principles and practices of place-based education. Students learn to design place-based curriculum and educative materials from an interdisciplinary analysis of specific places. Cross-listed with: EDTE 1610.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor Permission Required; Priority registration for students enrolled in PBE certificate. Then open to Degree and PACE students; Cross listed with EDTE 1610A; Combined enrollment = 25; Course Fee $25.00

nodes.title: Ecological Design & Living Tec
nodes.description: Explores the potential for ecological design to shape a sustainable future. Analyzes living technologies for food production, waste management and environmental restoration. Prerequisite: Minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Junior standing; Minimum Sophomore standing; Must register for lab.

nodes.title: Natural Hist & Human Ecology 1
nodes.description: Integrates the science of ecology and the science of humans and society to understand the relationship between the natural landscape's effects on society and social organization, and society's effects on the natural landscape.

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: VT: Natural & Cultural Hst
nodes.description: Introduction to the Vermont landscape that combines elements of natural history, field ecology, and environmental history. Students visit locations around the Champlain Valley as they build observational skills, study natural systems, and examine past and present human relationships with nature.

nodes.title: Nature and Mental Health
nodes.description: Special topics in natural resources beyond the scope of existing formal courses.

nodes.title: Ecological Restoratio Politics
nodes.description: See Schedule of Courses for specific titles.

nodes.title: Environmental Policy
nodes.description: Exploration of policy aspects of environment and natural resources including policy processes, public governance, and citizen participation with applications to environmental issues. Prerequisite: NR 2040 or POLS 1300.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Sophomore standing; Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Ecology and Evolution
nodes.description: Ecosystem and community structure, population growth, species interactions and niche dynamics, population and chromosomal genetics, speciation in fossil records, ecology of animal behavior, applied ecology. Prerequisites: BCOR 1400, BIOL 1400, or BIOL 1000; BCOR 1450, BIOL 1450, BIOL 1005, or BCOR 1425; MATH 1212 or MATH 1234.
nodes.other_requirements: PACE students with permission and override.

nodes.title: General Chemistry 1
nodes.description: First semester of a two-semester sequence. Topics include matter, stoichiometry, gas laws, thermochemistry, quantum theory, atomic structure, electronic configurations, bonding, and intermolecular forces. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1400 and CHEM 1100, CHEM 1102, or CHEM 1405.

nodes.title: Kincentric Ecology
nodes.description: 'Kincentric ecology', as defined by Enrique Salmon, is when humans view themselves as part of an extended ecological family that shares ancestry and origins with other species. Explores how we relate to other species through engagement with a number of different fields, including ecology, evolution, ecofeminism, multispecies ethnography, and Indigenous Place-Thought. Prerequisite: Sophomore standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Sophomore standing. Minimum Sophomore standing

nodes.title: Outline: Organic & BIOC w/lab
nodes.description: Broad overview of most important facts and principles of organic and biochemistry and interrelationships between these branches of chemistry. Credit not awarded for both CHEM 1150 and CHEM 1152, CHEM 1580, or CHEM 1582. Prerequisite: CHEM 1100 or CHEM 1400.
nodes.other_requirements: Must register for a CHEM 1150 lab, L01 - L06; $85 fee: Chem Lab Supplies; Open to Degree and PACE students; Cross listed with CHEM 1152A; total enrollment cap = 142

nodes.title: Internship
nodes.description: On-site supervised work experience combined with a structured academic learning plan directed by a faculty member or a faculty-staff team in which a faculty member is the instructor of record, for which academic credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required. Offered at department discretion.

nodes.title: Traditional Ecol. Knowledge
nodes.description: Examines the diversity and complexity of Traditional Ecological Knowledge systems and their role in shaping human-environment relationships. Students explore concepts, theories, and ethnographic perspectives on TEK, emphasizing its integration with scientific and local knowledge for sustainability and resource management. Through lectures, discussions, and creative projects, the course fosters critical thinking about cultural diversity, ecological ethics, and global challenges, offering a holistic understanding of knowledge systems across scales and contexts. Prerequisite: Minimum Junior standing.
nodes.other_requirements: Minimum Junior standing

nodes.title: Independent Study
nodes.description: A course which is tailored to fit the interests of a specific student, which occurs outside the traditional classroom/laboratory setting under the supervision of a faculty member, for which credit is awarded. Offered at department discretion.
nodes.other_requirements: Instructor permission required and completion of an Experiential Learning plan required

nodes.title: US Political System
nodes.description: Institutions, processes, and problems of American government. Credit not awarded for both POLS 1300 and POLS 1013.

nodes.title: Exploring Biology 2
nodes.description: An evolutionary perspective to exploring biology. Topics include: patterns of inheritance, Darwinian evolution, evolution of biodiversity, ecology of organisms, human effects on biological systems. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1450 and BIOL 1450.

nodes.title: Stream Ecology
nodes.description: Ecology of streams including hydrodynamics, morphology, sediment transport, chemistry, biology and human impacts. Field and laboratory experience. Prerequisites: BIOL 1000 and BIOL 1005, or BIOL 1000 and BCOR 1425, or BIOL 1400 and BIOL 1450, or BCOR 1400 and BCOR 1450; CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1150, or CHEM 1400 and CHEM 1450; NR 2030 or BCOR 2100.
nodes.other_requirements: Prereq enforced by the system

nodes.title: Intro to Envrnmtl Studies
nodes.description: Survey of the interdisciplinary field of environmental studies. Examines ecological, cultural, historical, and political influences on the role of humans in local and global ecosystems and the grand challenges humanity faces. Required for Environmental Studies majors and minors.

nodes.title: Principles of Biology 2
nodes.description: Principles of organismal biology; nature of scientific inquiry, plant form and function, pollination ecology, animal phylogeny illustrated by comparative anatomy and physiology; animal behavior. Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1450 and BCOR 1450.
nodes.other_requirements: ASCI, BIOL, BISC, MGEN, MICR, NEUR, PBIO, ZOOL majors should register for BCOR 1450; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; Post Bac Pre med students register for BIOL 1450

nodes.title: Limnology
nodes.description: Ecology of lakes and reservoirs, including their origin, physics, chemistry and biology, and the effects of anthropogenic perturbations. Field and laboratory experience. Prerequisites: BIOL 1000 and BIOL 1005, or BIOL 1000 and BCOR 1425, or BIOL 1400 and BIOL 1450, or BCOR 1400 and BCOR 1450; CHEM 1100 and CHEM 1150, or CHEM 1400 and CHEM 1450; NR 2030 or BCOR 2100.
nodes.other_requirements: Course fee: $89. Open to Degree and PACE students.

nodes.title: Principles of Biology 1
nodes.description: Principles of cellular biochemistry; cell biology; genetics and evolution. Topics: biochemistry; metabolism, cell structure/function; respiration; photosynthesis; molecular, Mendelian and population genetics; genetics of evolution. Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1400 and BCOR 1400 or BCOR 1425.
nodes.other_requirements: Credit not awarded for both BIOL 1400 and BCOR 1400 or BCOR 1425. Must register for a BIOL 1400 lab; Lab supply fee: $30; Degree students only even after level restrictions removed; Post Bac Pre Med & PACE students register for BIOL 1400 ZRA Colocated with BIOL 1400 ZRA

nodes.title: Natural Hist & Human Ecology 2
nodes.description: Integrates the science of ecological sciences and the science of humans and society to understand the relationship between the natural landscape's effects on society and social organization, and society's effects on the natural landscape. Pre/Co-requisite: NR 1010.
nodes.other_requirements: Restricted to RSENR students only; Must register for a NR lab A01- A15; Course Fee: $110 field trips

nodes.title: Applied Sensory Ecology
nodes.description: Advanced special topics in natural resource planning beyond the scope of existing formal courses.

nodes.title: Exploring Biology 1
nodes.description: Exploring biology from cells to organisms. Topics include origins of life, ancestral organisms, uni- and multi- cellular energetics, evolution of respiration and metabolism, and the genetic code. Credit not awarded for both BCOR 1400 and BIOL 1400 or BCOR 1425.

nodes.title: Honors
nodes.description: Honors course.
nodes.other_requirements: Honors course. Instructor permission required

nodes.title: Intro Environmental Sci
nodes.description: Explores the complex interactions between humans and environmental systems and the ecological foundations and scientific principles to better understand how the coupled human-natural system works, and how science can be used to help solve environmental problems.
